title: Optimistic vs pessimistic locking, exactly 3 paragraphs
response:
Optimistic locking doesn't actually lock anything: each row carries a version number (or timestamp), you read the row along with its version, and when you write back you do so conditionally — `UPDATE ... SET version = version + 1 WHERE id = ? AND version = ?`. If zero rows were updated, someone else changed the record since you read it, and your application must retry or surface the conflict. Pessimistic locking takes a real lock at read time, typically `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE` inside a transaction, so any other transaction wanting that row blocks until you commit or roll back.

Optimistic failure: two support agents open the same ticket during a flash-sale outage; agent A saves, bumping version 5 to 6, so agent B's save fails — B retries, but a third edit lands first, and after several failed retries B's fifteen-minute writeup is rejected. Pessimistic failure: a user opens an edit form that takes a `FOR UPDATE` lock, then leaves for lunch; every other transaction touching that row hangs, connections pile up, and the pool exhausts.

Rule of thumb: measure contention. If concurrent edits to the same row are rare — say, under a few percent of writes — use optimistic locking and pay the occasional retry. If many writers routinely hit the same hot rows, retries will thrash, so take short-lived pessimistic locks instead, and never hold them across user think-time.
